text: The surgeon begins mobilizing the left colon using a medial to lateral approach, exposing the feeding artery and draining vein. The left ureter and gonadal vein are carefully preserved while the feeding vessels are clipped and severed.
The surgeon continues the lateral mobilization of the colon, extending the dissection up to the splenic flexure.
The surgeon enters the mesorectal fascia dorsally, carefully sparing the pelvic nerves, and continues the total mesorectal excision in a circular fashion.

A small incision is made in the lower left quadrant. The surgeon assesses perfusion traditionally by observing tissue color and palpating the pulse in the marginal artery. With the lights off, indocyanine green is administered intravenously, revealing sufficient perfusion through the green fluorescence visible in the colon and mesenteric vessels.
Following extracorporeal bowel division, the surgeon closes the small incision in the lower left quadrant and re-establishes the pneumoperitoneum.
Following a standard stapler anastomosis, the surgeon performs a leakage test, which is negative. Perfusion is reassessed using an endoscopic view, showing the colon and mesenteric vessels.
